contest.title: IAEPC Preliminary Contest (Codeforces Round 999, Div. 1 + Div. 2)

sources.notes: Statement and constraints; each forward operation merges x,y with |x-y|<=1 into x+y, and sequence equality is multiset equality.
Official hints explicitly point to reconstructing the two operands of a merged value and processing the operations in reverse.
Confirms reverse splitting with a max-heap, target frequencies, and the fragment-count bound that prevents excessive splitting.
Cross-checks that a merged value uniquely determines its two nearly equal operands and that largest-first multiset processing runs in O(n log n).
Proved the largest-first decisions are forced and exhaustively compared the greedy test with all forward-reachable multisets for n<=5 and a_i<=4.

reference_candidates.notes: Codeforces status confirms an accepted implementation; the source page was blocked by a browser challenge, so it was not used as evidence.

annotation.summary: Decide whether multiset a can be transformed into multiset b by repeatedly merging two values differing by at most one.
annotation.constraints: Up to 1e4 test cases; 1 <= m <= n <= 2e5, sum of n <= 2e5, and 1 <= a_i,b_i <= 1e9. Total sums require 64-bit arithmetic.
annotation.core_idea: Reverse the process. For a merged value x, the only unordered pair with sum x and difference at most one is {floor(x/2),ceil(x/2)}, so every reverse split is deterministic. First require sum(a)=sum(b). Keep unmatched a values in descending order (or a frequency map) and current fragments from b in a max-heap. Compare the maxima x from the heap and y from a: if x=y, matching them is forced because splitting only decreases values and no smaller fragment can later create y; if x<y, y can never be produced; if x>y, x matches no remaining target and must be split into floor(x/2) and ceil(x/2). A fragment 1 cannot be split. If the number of current fragments exceeds the number of unmatched targets, reject; splits only increase that count. These forced choices are both necessary and sufficient, and the count bound limits the process to at most n-m splits.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory per test case; O(sum n log(sum n)) across the input.
tricks: Reverse a many-choice merge process into a unique split process.
A value x has the unique valid reverse children floor(x/2) and ceil(x/2).
Always resolve the largest current fragment because future splits can only decrease values.
Stop when the current fragment count exceeds the number of unmatched target leaves.
Check the preserved total sum with 64-bit arithmetic.

solution_variants.name: largest-first-reverse-splitting
solution_variants.summary: Sort or count the target multiset a, place b in a max-heap, greedily match equal maxima, and uniquely split every oversized maximum until all targets are consumed or a forced impossibility is found.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: The forward merge has many choices, while reversing a value x produces the unique pair floor(x/2),ceil(x/2); the entire feasibility test is built on this reversal.
At every step the largest fragment has a forced action: match an equal largest target, split if it is larger, or reject if it is smaller. Splitting cannot create a larger value later.
A max-heap repeatedly extracts the current largest fragment and inserts its two reverse children while keeping each operation logarithmic.
